name: Fair Returns on Australian Gas Exports
content:
There is an ongoing push from advocates and crossbench politicians for a deep examination into the extraordinarily low rates of revenue Australians receive from the export of our gas resources.

Campaigners are calling for intense scrutiny on the Petroleum Resource Rent Tax (PRRT) paid on Liquified Natural Gas (LNG) and why it remains so low compared to policies in other jurisdictions like Norway and Qatar. A key focus of the advocacy highlights the disparity where gas companies effectively pay less for offshore LNG extraction than everyday Australians pay in beer excise.

The broader movement seeks to highlight the impact of rising gas prices on Australian businesses and households since 2016. Advocates argue that effectively taxing the offshore LNG industry would generate significant additional revenue.

Campaigners stress that Australians have had enough of multinational gas companies profiting off national resources without providing a fair return. They argue the country has a narrow window to capture the benefits of the LNG boom to invest in essential services like housing, health, and education.

Rather than squandering potential revenues while nations like Norway build trillion-dollar sovereign wealth funds, advocates are calling on governments of all political persuasions to prioritise fair taxation models that require big companies to pay their fair share.

name: Addressing the Long COVID and ME/CFS Crisis
content:
The Campaign:
Advocacy groups continue to work tirelessly to raise critical awareness about the growing needs of Australians living with Long COVID and Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS). The overarching goal of these ongoing campaigns is to break down barriers, support patients, and bring together parliamentarians and clinicians to advocate for stronger policy outcomes and coordinated research investment.

The Impact on Australia's Economy and Workforce:
Long COVID is a mass-disabling event that has dealt a severe blow to the Australian workforce. Research by major Australian universities highlights that Long COVID costs the national economy billions of dollars in lost productivity—accounting for a massive hit to real GDP growth. Workers experiencing ongoing symptoms result in roughly 100 million lost labour hours annually. The economic burden disproportionately falls on the prime working-age demographic, with 30- to 49-year-olds accounting for more than half of the lost productivity. Hundreds of thousands of Australians remain severely affected 12 months or more after their initial infection.

The Underfunding and Health System Crisis:
Despite the staggering economic and human cost, Long COVID and related post-viral conditions like ME/CFS remain severely underfunded in Australia compared to countries like the US and the UK. Historically, federal spending on post-viral syndrome research has equated to mere cents per patient. Because of this historic lack of investment, there are currently no scientifically validated treatments for Long COVID. The burden of care has fallen almost entirely on an already-strained General Practice system, which lacks the specific Medicare funding and dedicated clinical pathways required to support patients. Advocates are desperately calling for the government to prioritise research investment, improve disability support services, and recognise post-viral illness as a top public health priority.

name: Reforming Property Tax to Fix Housing Affordability
content:
The Housing Affordability Crisis:
Amidst a worsening housing affordability crisis and a widening wealth gap, advocates and economists are intensifying calls for structural property tax reform. For a generation of Australians, homeownership has slipped out of reach as the property market increasingly favours investors over first-home buyers. Campaigners argue that housing must be treated primarily as a basic human right, rather than a speculative vehicle for wealth accumulation.

Targeting Tax Concessions:
At the centre of this advocacy is the push to overhaul negative gearing and the Capital Gains Tax (CGT) discount. Advocates point out that these generous tax concessions artificially inflate property prices by encouraging investors to bid against owner-occupiers for existing homes. These policies currently cost the federal budget billions in foregone revenue annually, simultaneously widening the wealth gap by disproportionately benefiting high-income earners while locking younger and wage-reliant workers out of the market.

Shifting Investment to New Builds:
Rather than a complete removal of these concessions, a leading, targeted proposal advocates for restricting negative gearing and the CGT discount to newly built homes only. By redirecting these tax incentives away from existing properties, the policy aims to stimulate actual housing supply. Advocates argue that if investor capital is incentivised to fund new construction, it will help alleviate the national housing shortage rather than fuelling bidding wars over the established housing stock.

Levelling the Playing Field:
Advocates stress that realigning these tax settings is essential to addressing structural economic inequality. By cooling artificial investor demand and redirecting funds toward building new homes, the government can ease the housing affordability crisis and ensure that the Australian dream of homeownership is accessible to those relying on wages, rather than just existing wealth.

name: Opposing Australian Involvement in Iran
content:
The Push for Peace:
In response to escalating global tensions, a broad coalition of peace advocates, human rights organisations, and concerned citizens are mounting a strong campaign urging the Australian government to firmly refuse any military or logistical support for armed conflict in Iran. Campaigners are calling on leaders to learn from the devastating legacies of past Middle Eastern interventions, which resulted in catastrophic loss of civilian life and decades of regional instability.

Upholding International Law:
Advocates emphasize that backing military action without an absolute, undeniable mandate under international law undermines global peace and stability. The movement stresses that Australia must champion diplomatic resolutions, de-escalation, and humanitarian aid over military force. Citizens are lobbying parliamentarians to ensure that Australia does not become complicit in what legal experts and human rights groups are warning would be an illegal and disastrous war.

A Call for an Independent Foreign Policy:
The campaign also highlights the need for Australian foreign policy to remain independent and aligned with the nation’s democratic values, rather than being automatically drawn into the military ventures of allied nations. Advocates are demanding a commitment to transparency, rigorous parliamentary debate before any deployment of military resources, and a steadfast dedication to preserving peace.

name: Withdrawing from the AUKUS Security Pact
content:
Re-evaluating National Security:
A growing movement of defense analysts, environmentalists, and grassroots activists is calling for Australia's immediate withdrawal from the AUKUS security pact. Central to the advocacy is the deep concern that acquiring nuclear-powered submarines binds Australia too closely to foreign military strategies, critically undermining the nation’s sovereign capacity to make independent foreign policy and defense decisions.

The Economic and Social Cost:
Campaigners heavily scrutinise the staggering financial commitment required by the AUKUS agreement, which is projected to cost Australian taxpayers hundreds of billions of dollars over the coming decades. During a severe cost-of-living and housing crisis, advocates argue that this astronomical sum should be urgently redirected toward critical domestic priorities. There is widespread demand to invest these funds into affordable housing, healthcare, education, and accelerating the transition to renewable energy.

Environmental and Regional Concerns:
Beyond the economic impact, the movement raises serious environmental and geopolitical alarms. Advocates oppose the establishment of nuclear waste facilities on Australian soil and warn that the pact accelerates an unnecessary regional arms race in the Asia-Pacific. Instead of militarisation, campaigners are urging the government to pursue peaceful diplomacy, strengthen regional alliances through humanitarian and economic cooperation, and maintain Australia’s long-standing commitment to nuclear non-proliferation.
